_patch_meta.description: Dimension review patch — C09: 125 dimension updates (boastfulness, hardness, pride, rebellion, stubbornness, weakness, sloth); 1 Sin & Vice correction; 7 Session B findings; 1 Session D pointer

record.finding: The ka.vod (glory) vocabulary in Reg 15 (boastfulness) contains its own internal corrective: divine glory (1861-001) as the proper object of inner awe, and the human act of directing glory toward God (1861-002) as the antidote to boastfulness that claims glory for itself. Session B should examine how the glory vocabulary frames the proper and improper objects of inner honour — why the same word (kavod) names both human boastfulness and divine majesty, and what this reveals about the inner-being structure of worship.

operations.description: Session B pointer — boastfulness/kavod: divine glory as corrective to human glory-seeking

record.finding: Registry 123 (pride) carries the most analytically complex dual-register in the cluster: human pride and divine majesty share the same vocabulary roots (ga.ah, ga.on, rum, ra.mam). The kauchaomai (26-001/002/003) groups make the structure explicit: legitimate boasting is directed toward God (Spiritual/God-ward); illegitimate boasting turns inward or toward self (Character/Disposition). The biblical claim is that pride is the human attempt to claim for oneself what belongs to God's majesty alone. Session B should examine the pride/majesty dual-register as a structured theological claim about the relationship between divine transcendence and human self-assertion.

operations.description: Session B pointer — pride: pride/majesty dual-register as theological claim about divine transcendence and human self-assertion

record.finding: Group 26-003 (Paul's paradoxical boasting in weakness — sustained inner act of inverting normal pride to reveal divine power) is analytically distinctive. This is not the absence of pride but its structural transformation: weakness embraced as the vehicle through which God's power is displayed. This group bridges C09 (pride/boasting) and C08 (Reg 170 weakness). Session B should examine this as a distinct inner-being posture: the voluntary inversion of the pride-orientation as itself a form of God-ward inner act.

operations.description: Session B pointer — pride: paradoxical boasting in weakness as God-ward inner posture (bridges C09 pride and C08 weakness)

record.finding: The sur (to turn aside) sub-gloss groups in Reg 128 show a consistent pattern: turning aside can be from God (6076-001 — Spiritual/God-ward negative), from idols toward God (6075-001 — Spiritual/God-ward positive), or toward God (6076-003 — Spiritual/God-ward positive). The same volitional act (turning) is the mechanism for both rebellion and repentance — only the direction differs. Session B should examine whether the turning vocabulary illuminates rebellion as fundamentally a directional problem of the inner being, and what this implies for understanding repentance as the reversal of the same inner movement.

operations.description: Session B pointer — rebellion/sur: turning as directional inner-being mechanism for both rebellion and repentance

record.finding: Registry 170 (weakness) is analytically anomalous within C09 (vice cluster). The weakness vocabulary in Paul shows a structured theological inversion: weakness is simultaneously a limitation (Affective/Emotional, 1219-001) and the privileged inner-being condition in which divine power is revealed (Theological/Divine-Human, 1219-002; 6639-003), God elects (Theological/Divine-Human, 6639-003), and Christ's pattern is enacted. Session B should examine weakness as a paradoxical inner-being category and ask whether it belongs analytically within C09 or whether it represents a distinct inner-being posture better addressed in relation to C08 (endurance) or C15 (faith/trust).

operations.description: Session B pointer — weakness: paradoxical category — limitation and grace-context; analytically anomalous within C09

record.finding: Group 1374-001 (the sluggard whose desire craves but whose will refuses to act) names sloth's inner-being structure precisely: not the absence of desire but the uncoupling of desire from volitional action. This is the inner-being gap that defines sloth — desire without will. Session B should examine whether the sloth vocabulary in Reg 208 consistently identifies this desire/will gap, or whether sloth is also described as the absence of desire itself, and what this reveals about the relationship between desire, will, and action in the biblical anthropology.

operations.description: Session B pointer — sloth: desire/will gap as sloth's defining inner-being structure

record.finding: Group 6077-002 in Reg 128 (the orphaned group from DIM-128-001) has anchor=0 and related=0. The four orphaned verses are: Deu 4:9 (warning not to turn aside from what was seen), Deu 7:4 (intermarriage turning sons from following God), 2Ki 3:3 (not departing from the sin of Jeroboam), Num 16:26 (command to depart from tents of Korah). Session B analyst should review whether any meet the inner-being filter for rebellion and should be reassigned to 6077-001 or 6077-003, or whether a distinct apostasy group is warranted. (Continuing DIM-128-001 instruction.)

operations.description: Session B pointer — rebellion/sur 6077-002: orphaned verses review (continues DIM-128-001)

record.description: The weakness-strength reversal (Reg 170: 6642-002, 1219-002) and the boasting-in-weakness posture (Reg 123: 26-003) together form a coherent Pauline inner-being theology of paradoxical inversion: strength through weakness, glory through shame, wisdom through foolishness. Session D should examine whether this inversion pattern is a cross-cluster theme requiring synthesis — relating C08 (endurance, strength), C09 (pride, weakness), and potentially C15 (faith/trust).

operations.description: Session D pointer — paradoxical inversion theme: weakness/strength reversal across Reg 170 (C09) and Reg 123 (C09) and C08
